title: Axios next
body:
Over the next few months I will be working on a new version of Axios, a v1 release bringing a vast amount of modernisations and bringing the library up to date with modern JS practices.

I would like people to please add any suggestions they have to this mega thread for discussion.

During this period I will also start closing any issues/pull requests that are related to future proposals or new features that are not backward compatible.

author: dbidwell94

title: Making request authorization simple.
body: A feature I'd like to see in Axios is an easy and automated way to handle some complex authorization types like digest auth. For reference, Postman has a very simple way for testing requests with a variety of auth models; but other than Postman (and maybe other similar apps for testing requests) I haven't seen any major, popular and active package to simplify authorization for http requests.
author: 8ctavio

title: requesting a 0.27.3 release of axios
body:
When the error handling was changed in 0.27 (refactoring into AxiosError), we lost stack traces.  The latest published, 0.27.2, has that regression, but it has been fixed in the latest code.

@jasonsaayman Would it be possible to do a 0.27.3 release to bring back stack traces on axios exceptions?

title: `onUploadProgress` doesn't work when `data` is a stream
body:
Related: https://github.com/axios/axios/issues/961

So I use a stream (from a file) as `data` in an Axios request in order to `POST` the file bytes as request body (required by that endpoint), so no multipart/form body.

This works fine now in recent Axios.

However, the `onUploadProgress` is never called. The reason for this is probably that a stream is used, and not a buffer or `FormData` with the file added to it.
As those files are larger, it would be nice to stream them anyway.

The `content-length` and `Content-Length` headers are correctly set as Axios request header.

With Axios v1.x we do get this awesome `runWhen` option to run request interceptors conditionally. I'm proposing the following 2 ideas that I can easily contribute myself 🤞

- Adding 2 booleans `skipRequestInterceptors` and `skipResponseInterceptors` that defaults to `false` to `AxiosRequestConfig`. body:
Hello,

The README says that [the default value for `config.responseType` is `json`](https://github.com/axios/axios/blob/v1.x/README.md?plain=1#L408).

Which is true, since `response.data` is an object, when the server replies with JSON.

But, `response.data` is also a string, when `config.responseType` is either `undefined` or `json`, despite the server replying with anything but JSON.

That behavior is welcome, but I don't see where in either of [`http`](https://github.com/axios/axios/blob/v1.x/lib/adapters/http.js) or [`xhr`](https://github.com/axios/axios/blob/v1.x/lib/adapters/xhr.js) adapters this is specified ?

As I'm writing [an adapter for Tauri](https://git.kaki87.net/KaKi87/axios-tauri-adapter) and forgot to reproduce this behavior, I'd like to know exactly is it made in official adapters, and if there's other logic I should know about, in order to be as accurate as possible to the original `axios` experience.

Additionally, I think it would be interesting to make dev-oriented resources on how to make adapters in general (especially for preserving the original features and experience), as I found none and had to improvise it, like anyone who ever done that I guess.

title: Allow custom adapters to selectively delegate some calls to the default adapter
body:
I maintain the [axios-response-mock](https://github.com/teetotum/axios-response-mock) library which provides a custom adapter to answer some requests with dummy data (and not hit the network); all unmatched requests passthrough normally (This is quite similar to -but not as popular as- [axios-mock-adapter](https://github.com/ctimmerm/axios-mock-adapter)).

Version 1.2.0 of axios introduced a change [`#5277`](https://github.com/axios/axios/pull/5277) (Changelog entry: `refactor: allowing adapters to be loaded by name #5277`) that makes it impossible to delegate any calls to the default adapter. Both mock libraries are affected and can no longer passthrough any requests if used with axios v1.2.0 or above.

I would like to discuss how axios could allow this use-case ("delegating calls to the default adapter") in a viable, clean, and documented way to enable utility libraries like `axios-mock-adapter` and `axios-response-mock` to chain their own adapter infront of the default adapter.

The following section contains my analysis and thoughts.

## Analysis

- in axios v 1.1.3 (and below) the original default adapter is a function
- (in axios v 1.2.0-alpha.1 the original default adapter is still a function)
- in axios v 1.2.0 (and above) the original default adapter is an array `['xhr', 'http']`

the change was introduced with [`#5277`](https://github.com/axios/axios/pull/5277)
(Changelog entry: `refactor: allowing adapters to be loaded by name #5277`)
(merged November 22nd) and released with v1.2.0 on November 22nd

If a custom adapter now wants to selectively decide which requests to handle itself and which requests to passthrough to the default adapter / delegate to the default adapter, how best would it now obtain a reference to the default adapter?

Before the change, a custom adapter could secure a reference to the default adapter via `axiosInstance.defaults.adapter` and call it later to delegate.

Example:

```
this.originalAdapter = axiosInstance.defaults.adapter;
axiosInstance.defaults.adapter = this._processRequest.bind(this);

// and later
if (matchedRoute)
    return this._respond(matchedRoute, config);
else
    return this.originalAdapter(config);
```

There is a related [feature request](https://github.com/axios/axios/pull/5324) albeit it seems rather unilaterally done and without consent or discussion with the axios maintainers.
It seems others are also in need of this https://github.com/Gerhut/axiosist/issues/55

title: Can a single Axios Instance be cached and used to make multiple http calls in parallel in a Node API Server?
body:
Can I cache a single Axios Instance and use it to make multiple calls to varied endpoints?

The thinking is that having set up the Axios Instance with an Auth Bearer Token, set its Timeout and other config, then to avoid having to do this setup again for every new http call, that we cache the single Axios Instance in a module on the API Server, and make all subsequent http requests using this instance.

Then we would be having calls in parallel being made using the same single Axios Instance.

Is that ok, and is the Axios Instance designed to be able to work in that way, or might we run into timing or contention errors, and so are we then better off making a new Axios Instance for each new incoming http request to the API Server?

title: What is the rationale for defining a second paramter between Response and Request type?
body:
### Describe the issue

The post method has the following type:

```typescript
post<T = any, R = AxiosResponse<T>, D = any>(url: string, data?: D, config?: AxiosRequestConfig<D>): Promise<R>;
```

The infered type ` R = AxiosResponse<T>` must be the 3rd paramter since the `T` (Response Type) and `D` (Request Parameters Type) are sufficient to fix the fully determine.

### Example Code

Currently, if we wish to define the body data type as well, we have to define 3 types. The 2nd parameter should be infered

```typescript
 axios.post<PostResponse, AxiosResponse<PostResponse> , PostBody>("/api/auth/login", { email, password })
      .then(({ data: user }) => console.log(user))
```


### Expected behavior

The ordering of the paramters must change:

```typescript
post<T = any, D = any, R = AxiosResponse<T>>(url: string, data?: D, config?: AxiosRequestConfig<D>): Promise<R>;
```

so that only two types are required to strongly fix the type and ignore the infered type.

```typescript
 axios.post<PostResponse, PostBody>("/api/auth/login", { email, password })
      .then(({ data: user }) => console.log(user))
```

title: Is Axios 1.x considered stable yet? If not is Axios 0.x still going to be in support in case of vulnerability?
body:
Hey there :wave: 

In attempting to upgrade to Axios 1.x without a migration guide, I feel like I've been running into complete blockers. I would be more than down to start working around those blockers if there was validation that Axios in its current state _is_ stable, but it seems like a lot of the 1.x releases have been primarily composed of fixes to functionality that was broken in the initial 1.x release. Without proper documentation on what the breaking changes are, it's hard to determine what is a bug that is in the process of being resolved, and what is just a set change of Axios 1.x that will need to be worked around regardless of future stability.

In the case that Axios 1.x is not stable, upgrading without an upgrade guide seems unnecessarily Sisyphean. I would love to wait for confirmed stability, but that also raises concerns with whether or not Axios 0.x will still be in support in the case of something like a critical vulnerability arising. 

A [comment](https://github.com/axios/axios/issues/5114#issuecomment-1277169905) in a closed issue (#5114) from October stated that Axios 1.x was _not_ stable, and that Axios 0.x _would_ continue to be in support, but the same comment stated that it would be receiving an update soon, which it hasn't, and I haven't been able to find anything on 1.x stability/0.x support since then (the last comment in the issue has been unaddressed for two weeks).

Would appreciate having this cleared up so that my team and I can determine next steps for our app. Thanks!

title: Why is there no support for Object header type?
body:
Extends the discussion here https://github.com/axios/axios/discussions/5616
Except with a trivial example.

"any" type, which extends Object
```javascript
const my_header = { "Authorization" : `Bearer ${token}` }

const config = {
    headers: my_header,
    timeout: 2000 //ms
}
const response = axios.post(url, body, config);
```

Object type
```javascript
const my_header = new Object();
my_header["Authorization"] = `Bearer ${token}`

const config = {
    headers: my_header,
    timeout: 2000 //ms
}
const response = axios.post(url, body, config);
```
Will show this error
```
Argument of type '{ headers: Object; timeout: number; }' is not assignable to parameter of type 'AxiosRequestConfig<FormData>'.
  Types of property 'headers' are incompatible.
    Type 'Object' is not assignable to type 'AxiosHeaders | (Partial<RawAxiosHeaders & { Accept: AxiosHeaderValue; "Content-Length": AxiosHeaderValue; "User-Agent": AxiosHeaderValue; "Content-Encoding": AxiosHeaderValue; Authorization: AxiosHeaderValue; } & { ...; }> & Partial<...>)'.
```

title: Interaction between proxy defined via `proxy` and `httpsAgent` settings
body:
I had a situation with an interesting interaction between a proxy defined via the `proxy` config setting and a proxy defined by the `httpsAgent` config setting (using [https-proxy-agent](https://www.npmjs.com/package/https-proxy-agent)).

The `httpsAgent` was intentional.

The the `proxy` config setting was unintentional and coming from an `HTTPS_PROXY` environment variable setting.

With both defined, requests would always fails with the error:
```
Error: socket hang up
```

The solution was to specify  the `proxy: false` setting to "block" the effect of any environment variables.

My questions:

1. What should be expected in this case? i.e. will it always fail in this manner
2. Is there a way that this config "overlap" can be detected? (I suspect it would be hard, though perhaps at runtime?)

I'm interested in anyone elses experience.

title: Prevent secret logging
body:
Heyo

I'm using Axios in my backend, and sometimes there are a few errors. Nothing problematic since everything is in try-catch clauses, but I realised that logging the trace for Axios can leak secrets, so I'm only logging the trace when in dev.

I'm curious if there is a way to log this trace while preventing leaking secrets. Python and FastAPI, for example, had a very handy "secret" type that would replace the string with "******" whenever it was logged.

Is there similar functionality in Axios? A way to indicate a certain variable is a secret and should not be logged in the trace?

Note also that I'm unfortunately dealing with a lot of secrets, so a solution like Python above would be much easier than a bespoke catch clause for every API call I'm making
author: MaelAbgrall

title: Why Axios throws exceptions for status code not equal to 200?
body: As in the title of the discussion, why Axios throws exceptions in this way? With this approach, we need to catch each status not equal to 200. Usually, the mapping of status codes for responses should not be handled by the http client. The application should do it. Moreover, Exceptions are really important when developing the application and catching random exceptions makes no sense (always in my opinion). I see this behavior as an abuse of exceptions

title: react-native, axios, and aws application load balancer
body:
I'm posting here since not sure where else to start and given the clues shown below it seems the most sensible place to start query.

We are sending a cookie using Axios to our API something like this:

```
axios.post(url, { ... some data ... }, { 
	headers: { Cookie: "my_cookie=foo" }
});
```

and in the backend we get a corrupted header that looks something like this. 

```
cookie: AWSLB=abc; AWSOTHER=def; AWSOTHER2=ghi,my_cookie=foo
```

This is just an example, we have other cases where get complete absurd cookie content like something relating to Shopify:

<details>

```
_S=69714497-bba2-4465-a668-a47fd809df79;
_shopify_s=6xxxxxxx-bba2-4465-a668-a47fd809df79;
_shopify_y=4xxxxxxx-fb6d-4a24-8a9a-49266745£188; _y=4241c653-fb6d-4a24-8a9a-4926
6745£188; _tracking_consent=87B%22region2283A822PH0082282C822reg82283A82282282C822con%2283A87B%22CMP%2283A87B%22a82283A822%2282C822582283A82282282C822p82283A82282282C822m&2283A8
22%2287087082C822v82283A8222.182272C8221im%2283A85B%22GDPR_BLOCK_ALL%22%5D7D,my_cookie=foo
```
</details>

What we have looked into:
1. the cookie value doesn't include a leading comma `,`
2. that our API doesn't mess up the cookie in decoding or otherwise. Used `ncat` to inspect incoming headers which indeed are corrupted before reaching the app.
3. tried with and without stickiness in the application load balancer, no difference
4. tried disabling and enabling removal of bad headers

Are there a way to inspect the headers (e.g. in intercepter) right before axios sends the request?

Also note we are running in react-native with some extensions like Didomi, Firebase, Amplitude that could maybe alter the request before sending to ensure GDPR because we do not add this ourselves. I'm not aware of this, just speculation. 

Other feedback is welcome. Thanks

ps: we are in communication with AWS regarding the load-balancer but so far no result.

title: Can I catch error within interceptor and ultimately return results?
body:
Greetings... 

I'm working within a very constricted environment and codebase, and have a weird requirement where in some cases we'll get a specific error from our API. In response to this error, I'd like to effectively handle the error, execute other logic, and ultimately return items as expected.

If status code 400 was a regular response (in the context of the interceptor), it'd be easy enough to get status code, run other logic, and ultimately resolve promise with the other data, but is there a way to do that from within response interceptors, onErrror?

I could probably have some layer wrapping, and have catches that ultimately return the same thing, but I'd like developers to be able to effectively be able to get a axios instance with interceptors, and use it as they are expected to after that.

1. Is there a way to intercept an error, and "handle" it in such a way that once handled, the get/post/whatever acts as if it never saw an error?
2. Is there a way where we can specify some conditions or an expression that determines whether a specific response types are considered to be an error or not?

Deeper detail
- If we call the api and a specific authentication problem occurs, a 400 code is returned with information required to resolve it.
- We make a call that gives us extra information, and ultimately call the same url with extra parameters
- The new call signs in and returns the data from the original request

All I'm hoping to do is be able to do `getSomeAxiosInstance().get(/* usual stuff here */)` (as a side note, I looked into axios-retry... as it kind of seems like what I was hoping to do, except other than retry, I just execute some logic and re-execute the endpoint once)

title: What is the difference between headers set in axiosInstance.default.headers and in axiosInstance.default.headers.common?
body:
While setting an Accept header for an AxiosInstance, I noticed that when the headers are passed initially via `const instance = axios.create({headers: {Accept: 'application/json'}})`, the header will be set under `instance.defaults.headers.Accept`. 

But wherever I read about overwriting default headers of axios it always mentions overwriting the header under `instance.defaults.headers.common` (see also https://www.npmjs.com/package/axios#custom-instance-defaults). Moreover, whether I call `axios.create({headers: {Accept: 'application/json'}})` or `axios.create()`, the value of `instance.defaults.headers.common.Accept` will be 'application/json, text/plain, */*', so the headers passed during create do not influence what's under common. I can only manually overwrite it with `instance.defaults.headers.common = {Accept: 'application/json'}`

So which one takes precedence? `instance.defaults.headers.Accept` or `instance.defaults.headers.common.Accept`? I know that if I set it for example directly for `instance.defaults.headers.post.Accept`, then this one will take precedence because it is more specific.

Or is this a bug in axios 1.7.7 and `instance.defaults.headers.Accept` should not even be possible?

title: Addressing Node 24 DEP0169 warnings (url.parse) in dependencies
body:
## The Issue

Node 24 now explicitly warns against `url.parse()` via `DEP0169`. This is causing significant log noise in production for Axios users, primarily coming from:

1.  **`proxy-from-env`** (already addressed in PR #7416)
2.  **`follow-redirects`** (tracked in [follow-redirects#283](https://github.com/follow-redirects/follow-redirects/issues/283))

## The Coordination Goal

The `follow-redirects` team is hesitant to migrate to the WHATWG `new URL()` API because they don't want to break compatibility for Axios. 

To help unblock them, I'd like to confirm the Axios team's stance:

1. **Target Node Range:** While CI tests Node 12, would Axios be open to dependency bumps that require Node 18+ to resolve these deprecations? 
2. **Strategy:** If we need to keep Node 12 support, would you prefer a conditional internal shim (using `new URL()` when available) or should we push for a dual-mode release in the upstream dependencies?

Just looking for a "green light" on the intended support range so I can relay that back to the `follow-redirects` maintainers. Happy to help with the PRs once the direction is clear!
